topic: Concurrency - JoinHandle and Thread Panic Propagation

turns.content: I'm trying to port an old C project that used threads, but I keep thinking Rust's thread::spawn is just a wrapper around pthreads. I assume if I call join() on the Handle it will automatically catch any panics from the worker and return them in the Result so I don't have to worry about them at all.

That's a common assumption when coming from C, but Rust handles panics differently than most other languages. When you spawn a thread with thread::spawn, it returns a JoinHandle<T>, and calling join() on that handle does indeed return a Result with the panic payload if one occurred in the worker. However, this only works for threads created via thread::spawn; detached threads don't participate in this mechanism because they are intentionally dropped without a Handle to retrieve results from.

Oh, so I can't just assume all spawned threads behave the same way? What happens if I spawn multiple threads and one panics while another completes successfully? Does join() for each handle still work independently?

Yes, you are correct that they operate independently. If you have multiple JoinHandles, calling join() on each one will return a Result for that specific thread's execution status regardless of what happened in other threads. The panic payload from one thread won't affect the successful completion of another unless you explicitly propagate it through some custom logic.
That makes sense, but I'm confused about unwrapping the Result. If I just call .unwrap() on join(), what happens if a panic occurred in that thread? Will the panic be re-raised or swallowed?

If you unwrap() a Result where the error is a panic payload without handling it, Rust will indeed re-raise that panic from the main thread. This means your program won't terminate gracefully; instead, it will crash with the same panic message as the worker thread did.
So I should never just unwrap()? What's the idiomatic way to handle this in a production app? Should I use expect() or map over the Result?

In idiomatic Rust, you typically avoid unwrapping panics entirely by using Option<T> instead of Result for cases where absence is acceptable. For join(), since it returns Result<JoinHandle::T>, you usually match on the Result explicitly to handle panic payloads gracefully or propagate them intentionally depending on your error handling strategy.
Wait, what about detached threads? If I want a thread that runs forever and doesn't need its result, can I just drop the Handle? Will panics in those threads still be caught?

Detached threads are created by passing JoinHandle::new() to std::thread::spawn with Detached flag. In this case, the panic payload is NOT returned because there's no Handle to retrieve it from. The thread will run until completion or panic, but the OS will handle cleanup and log any panics instead of returning them to your main thread.
So detached threads are basically fire-and-forget? That means I lose visibility into whether they crashed?

Exactly. Detached threads are fire-and-forget, so you lose visibility into crashes unless you implement logging or monitoring externally. This is useful for background tasks where you don't need the result, but be careful not to use detached for critical paths where failure should halt your application.
